skill.name: hig-inputs
skill.description: Apple Human Interface Guidelines guidance for input methods and interaction patterns across Apple platforms, including gestures, keyboards, pointers, Apple Pencil, game controllers, Digital Crown, eye tracking, remotes, motion sensors, spatial interactions, nearby interactions, and Camera Control.
skill.summary: Apply Apple HIG input guidance across touch, keyboard, pointer, Pencil, controller, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS experiences.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but reviewed examples are Markdown documentation, Apple Developer URLs, image links, API names in backticks, and UI words such as Command and Control. No executable scripts, command invocation, data exfiltration, malicious network behavior, or prompt injection instructions were found in the reviewed skill files.

low_findings.title: Local Design Context File Read
low_findings.description: SKILL.md instructs the assistant to check .claude/apple-design-context.md before asking questions. This is a bounded local project-context read, but it should remain visible because it can cause workspace file access during normal use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and limited to one local design-context path. It does not request broad file enumeration, secret access, or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The reference files contain many Apple Developer and Apple asset URLs. They are Markdown source and image links used for citations and examples, not code that initiates network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed URL hits appear in Markdown frontmatter, image syntax, and ordinary reference links. No fetch, curl, upload, or runtime network logic was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Backtick API Names
dangerous_patterns.description: Static external-command findings are caused by Markdown inline code for Apple APIs and symbols, such as UIKit, SwiftUI, and AppKit names. These lines are documentation and do not execute shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are Markdown formatting around API or keyboard-symbol names. The repository contains Markdown guidance, not Ruby, shell, JavaScript, or executable scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: UI and Asset Terminology
dangerous_patterns.description: C2, screen capture upload, weak crypto, and reconnaissance hits map to UI words, screenshot descriptions, hashed Apple asset URLs, and keyboard shortcut tables. The semantic context is Apple HIG documentation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed examples describe viewfinder layout, keyboard screenshot shortcuts, and WWDC video links. No malware command-and-control, cryptographic implementation, or upload routine is present.

content.value_statement: Apple platform input rules are detailed and vary by device. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code apply Apple HIG guidance to touch, keyboard, pointer, Pencil, controller, motion, and spatial input designs.
actual_capabilities: Recommends platform-appropriate input methods for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS.
Summarizes HIG guidance for gestures, Apple Pencil, Scribble, keyboards, pointers, remotes, and controllers.
Helps define gesture behavior, keyboard shortcuts, focus navigation, and accessibility alternatives.
Highlights platform constraints for Camera Control, Digital Crown, nearby interactions, and motion sensors.
Provides reference-backed guidance for avoiding conflicts with system gestures, overlays, and standard shortcuts.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not generate tested application code by itself.
It relies on included Markdown references and may not include future Apple HIG updates.
It does not replace testing with real devices, assistive technologies, and platform simulators.
It covers input design patterns, not complete visual design systems or app architecture.

use_cases.title: Plan Cross-Platform Input Support
use_cases.description: Map touch, pointer, keyboard, controller, and accessibility inputs for an Apple app before implementation.

use_cases.title: Review Interaction Specifications
use_cases.description: Check whether proposed gestures, shortcuts, and focus behavior follow Apple platform expectations.

use_cases.title: Prepare Implementation Notes
use_cases.description: Create developer-ready guidance for Apple Pencil, Camera Control, Digital Crown, or visionOS input behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Input Methods
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this feature for Apple platforms and recommend the input methods it should support. Include touch, keyboard, pointer, and accessibility alternatives where relevant.

prompt_templates.title: Design Gesture Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a gesture specification for this screen. Separate standard gestures from custom gestures, and note any Apple system gestures that must not be overridden.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Keyboard and Focus
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this interaction model for keyboard shortcuts, tab order, focus movement, and discoverability. Flag conflicts with standard Apple shortcuts.

prompt_templates.title: Build Platform Input Matrix
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a platform input matrix for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS. Include supported inputs, fallbacks, constraints, and testing notes.

output_examples.input: A drawing app wants Apple Pencil, finger gestures, and keyboard shortcuts on iPad.
output: Use Apple Pencil for marking, pressure, tilt, hover preview, and Scribble in text fields.
Keep finger gestures for navigation, panning, zooming, selection, and standard system behaviors.
Expose common keyboard shortcuts and avoid redefining standard editing commands.

output_examples.input: A visionOS app uses custom hand gestures in a Full Space.
output: Use standard look and pinch interactions for primary controls where possible.
Request hand data only when custom gestures are necessary for the experience.
Avoid placing custom hand content where it conflicts with system overlays.

output_examples.input: A media app needs Apple TV remote and game controller support.
output: Make every interactive element reachable through the focus system.
Keep Siri Remote interactions simple and predictable.
Offer sensible controller mappings and preserve remote or keyboard fallbacks.

best_practices: Start with standard Apple input patterns before adding custom gestures or shortcuts.
Design every interaction with at least one accessible alternative input path.
Validate input behavior on the actual target platform, not only in static mockups.
anti_patterns: Overriding system gestures, edge swipes, or standard keyboard shortcuts without a strong reason.
Making custom gestures the only way to complete an important task.
Copying one platform input model directly to another platform without adapting it.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps apply Apple HIG guidance to input methods, gestures, shortcuts, focus, controllers, Pencil, motion, and spatial interactions.

faq.question: Does it support visionOS input guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for eyes, hands, spatial interactions, focus, and pointing devices in visionOS.

faq.question: Can it design keyboard shortcuts?
faq.answer: Yes. It can recommend shortcut patterns and flag conflicts with standard Apple shortcuts.

faq.question: Does it generate production code?
faq.answer: No. It provides design and implementation guidance, but production code still needs platform-specific engineering and testing.

faq.question: Why does the security audit mention links?
faq.answer: The references include Apple Developer documentation and image links. They are static Markdown links, not executable network requests.

faq.question: Should teams still test with real devices?
faq.answer: Yes. Input behavior depends on hardware, accessibility settings, and platform conventions that require real testing.
